summary_one_line: A long Thai-glyph sequence that drives the above/below state machine to emit the RD action, reaching case RD

hypotheses.what_input_feature: An input long enough and structured so the Thai shaper's above/below state machine emits action == RD at line 256, which dispatches the switch into case RD at line 150; this requires a multi-glyph Thai mark sequence rather than any single literal byte value.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: Naive winning seeds are large (128-236 bytes) and built by byte-expansion/insert havoc (BytesExpandMutator, BytesInsertCopyMutator, BytesRandInsertMutator), carrying repeated 0e / 3a 0e 00 Thai-region byte runs that feed many state-machine transitions until action == RD fires; naive resolves the branch in all 10 trials (hitcount 37).
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: Cmplog blocking seeds are short (21-78 bytes) and lack the sustained Thai-mark sequence, so action == RD (line 256, L T=0) never fires and the switch never reaches case RD; cmplog stays blocked 10/10 trials with hitcount 0 for 21.5h.
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: The winner is naive, whose only edge is plain havoc/token byte mutation (no CMP introspection). The RD action is reached by accumulating enough Thai glyphs through the state machine, a quantitative/structural property that byte-expansion havoc stumbles onto. Cmplog's added CMP-operand substitution stage gives no advantage here; if anything cmplog's CMP-biased budget produced shorter inputs that under-drive the state machine, so naive's pure havoc wins.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocked side is case RD inside a switch on the action variable.
The decisive pair has naive as winner and cmplog as loser, delta I2S.
case RD is dispatched only when action == RD, which is gated on the state-machine edge at line 256.
The loser never takes the RD-action branch: line 256 is true 0 times in L versus 11 in W.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: d=2   L 256  T=11 F=26  T=0 F=15  if (action == RD)

evidence_trail.claim: Naive winning seeds are large and produced by byte-expansion/insert havoc mutators.
The loser remained blocked far longer with zero hits on the branch.
The winning fuzzer naive uses only havoc + token byte mutation with no I2S.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: Observed `mutation_op` in seed metadata**: any of the above. No I2S.

evidence_trail.claim: Cmplog's extra technique over naive is I2SRandReplace operating on CMP operands.
evidence_trail.exact_quote:
`I2SRandReplace` reads the post-execution `CmpLogObserver` buffer,
picks a CMP entry, scans the input for byte sequences matching one
operand, and substitutes the other operand at those offsets.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: claimed_mechanism is havoc-only, not I2SRandReplace, so lineage verification is not required. The winner is naive (no CMP introspection); this is an I2S-anti case where the I2S fuzzer (cmplog) loses, so the winning mechanism cannot be I2SRandReplace. The branch is reached by byte-expansion havoc accumulating a long Thai-glyph sequence that drives the state machine to emit action == RD.

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: A synthetic experiment in which cmplog reaches case RD as fast as or faster than naive (equal time-to-first-hit and equal final hit rate), which would show I2S substitution helps rather than that pure havoc length-growth is the determining factor.

weakest_evidence_point: The claim that input length / Thai-glyph accumulation (not a specific literal) is what gates RD relies on inference from seed sizes and the line-256 state-machine edge rather than a direct decode of which Thai codepoints in the winner seeds produce the RD edge.
